stories.headline: Man killed in downtown Denver shooting near 17th Street and Glenarm Place
stories.summary:
Denver police responded to a fatal shooting Saturday evening at approximately 6:43 p.m. near the intersection of 17th Street and Glenarm Place in downtown Denver. One man was pronounced dead at the scene; his identity is to be released by the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner.

Authorities said one suspect had been detained Saturday evening. Additional details about the events leading up to the shooting were not immediately available.

The incident adds to ongoing public safety concerns in the downtown Denver corridor. Police have not released information on charges or a motive.

stories.headline: One dead, Greeley police officer hospitalized after officer-involved shooting on 20th Street
stories.summary:
One person died and a Greeley police officer was transported to a hospital following a shooting on 20th Street in Greeley on Saturday morning, according to officials. Circumstances leading to the shooting were not immediately released.

The officer's condition was not specified in initial reports. The incident is under investigation by Greeley authorities.

Officer-involved shootings in Colorado are typically reviewed by the district attorney's office and, in many cases, the Colorado Attorney General's office under state law.

stories.headline: Colorado car thefts drop 34% in 2025, marking third consecutive year of decline
stories.summary:
Vehicle thefts in Colorado fell 34% from 2024 to 2025, according to a new report from the Colorado State Patrol's Auto Theft Intelligence Coordination Center. The decline marks the third straight year of decreasing thefts and represents a 56% reduction since 2021, when Colorado led the nation in per capita vehicle thefts.

The state recorded 16,291 car thefts in 2025, ranking 14th nationally in volume. Colorado's theft rate of 271 per 100,000 residents remains the sixth worst in the country, down significantly from more than 500 per 100,000 in 2021.

Hyundai and Kia vehicles accounted for roughly 15% of all Colorado thefts in 2025, with 2,445 combined stolen. Metro Denver counties — including Adams, Arapahoe, Broomfield, Denver, Douglas, Gilpin, and Jefferson — accounted for 69% of all statewide thefts.

State Patrol Col. Matt Packard urged drivers to lock vehicles and use available tools such as free steering wheel locks distributed at State Patrol offices through the Colorado Auto Theft Prevention Authority.

stories.headline: CBI issues missing person alert for two Indigenous children last seen in Denver
stories.summary:
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation has issued a missing person alert for two siblings last seen March 23 in the 3200 block of North Dahlia Street in Denver. Ariauna Brown, 12, and Andrew Brown, 15, have been missing for nearly a week; authorities say it is unknown what they were wearing when last seen.

Ariauna is described as a 5-foot, 100-pound Indigenous girl with brown hair and brown eyes. Andrew is a 5-foot-6-inch, 126-pound Indigenous boy with black hair and brown eyes. Law enforcement has expressed concern for their safety.

Anyone with information is asked to call the Denver Police Department at 720-913-2000 or dial 911.

stories.headline: Rockies swept in Miami to open season, lose all three games by one run
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies were swept in their opening series against the Miami Marlins, losing Sunday's finale 4-3 on a walk-off two-run home run by Owen Caissie off reliever Victor Vodnik in the ninth inning. Colorado dropped all three games by a single run — 2-1, 4-3, and 4-3 — marking only the second time in franchise history the team was swept in its opening series. The last occurrence was in 1994.

The Rockies hit just 3 for 20 (.150) with runners in scoring position across the series, including 1 for 12 on Sunday. Colorado took a 3-0 lead in the first inning on Jordan Beck's three-run double but was blanked the rest of the game, striking out 11 times.

On the positive side, the bullpen posted a 2.31 ERA over 11 2/3 innings for the series, and the Rockies stole five bases while being caught only once. Manager Warren Schaeffer emphasized that Colorado was competitive in all three games and has 159 remaining.

The sweep raises immediate concerns about clutch hitting as the team tries to avoid a repeat of last season's franchise-record 119 losses.

stories.headline: Nuggets beat Warriors for sixth straight win despite losing four forwards to injuries
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets defeated the Golden State Warriors 116-93 Sunday to extend their winning streak to six games, but the victory came at a significant cost. Four of the team's top five forwards — Aaron Gordon, Spencer Jones, Cam Johnson, and Zeke Nnaji — were sidelined or injured during the game, leaving coach David Adelman with a makeshift lineup.

Gordon was a precautionary scratch with left calf tightness. Jones exited after the first quarter with right hamstring tightness. Johnson left in the third quarter with back spasms and was listed as questionable. Nnaji, filling in at center, sustained left hip impingement after taking an elbow to the face and left the arena on crutches.

Adelman was forced to use Jonas Valanciunas — inactive for five straight games — alongside four guards to start the fourth quarter. Valanciunas saw his first action since March 18 as Denver held on and pulled away. The severity of Johnson's, Jones's, and Nnaji's injuries was not confirmed after the game, with MRI and X-ray results pending.

The injuries loom large with the playoffs approaching, though Denver's comfortable margin of victory allowed Johnson and others to avoid being pushed through potential aggravation of their conditions.

stories.headline: DU Pioneers reach third straight Frozen Four; coach David Carle improves to 14-3 in NCAA tournament
stories.summary:
The University of Denver Pioneers punched their ticket to a third consecutive Frozen Four with a 6-2 win over Western Michigan on Sunday in Loveland. Coach David Carle, 36, improved his NCAA tournament record to 14-3 — an 82.5% winning percentage — and will take the program to its sixth Frozen Four in his eight seasons leading the team.

The Pioneers navigated significant adversity this season, sitting at 12-9-2 on January 11 before going 15-2-1 over their final 18 games. A turning point came January 24 when freshman goaltender Johnny Hicks replaced the injured Quentin Miller, sparking a stretch of 14-0-1 hockey.

DU athletics vice chancellor Josh Berlo credited Carle's ability to manage injuries, elevated expectations, and roster changes while remaining competitive at the highest level of college hockey. The Pioneers are the first DU team to earn three consecutive Frozen Four berths.

Denver will next compete in Las Vegas at the Frozen Four, chasing what would be Carle's third national championship as head coach.

stories.headline: Colorado Democrats hold first in-person State Assembly since 2018 in Pueblo
stories.summary:
The Colorado Democratic Party convened its 2026 State Assembly in Pueblo on Saturday, March 28, drawing nearly 1,600 delegates. It was the first in-person assembly the party has held since 2018, following years of virtual or hybrid formats.

The assembly addressed candidate nominations and primary matchups for key statewide races. Results of delegate votes will shape the ballot for Colorado's June primary election.

The Pueblo gathering underscores the Democratic Party's effort to re-engage grassroots organizers ahead of a competitive 2026 election cycle in Colorado.

stories.headline: Denver's $1 billion traffic-calming overhaul would reduce car lanes, expand transit and bike infrastructure citywide
stories.summary:
Denver is advancing more than 500 traffic-calming projects across the city at a total cost approaching $1 billion, with the goal of improving pedestrian safety, expanding public transit, and reducing vehicle dominance on city streets. The initiative, rooted in complete streets guidelines adopted in 2020, includes the $280 million reconstruction of East Colfax Avenue with Bus Rapid Transit, scheduled for completion next year.

Major planned projects include lane reductions and bus priority improvements on West 38th Avenue, a BRT route along a 13-mile Speer Boulevard-to-Nine Mile corridor, reconstruction of the 90-year-old Eighth Avenue viaduct, and pedestrian and bike improvements along Santa Fe Drive and West Mississippi Avenue. Most projects remain in planning and design phases, with completion targeted by 2032.

Supporters argue the changes will reduce traffic fatalities and improve quality of life as Denver's population grows. Critics, including the Colorado Automobile Dealers Association, contend that narrowing roads will worsen congestion and increase emissions from idling vehicles.

Mayor Mike Johnston acknowledged that reducing lanes will slow traffic on affected streets but said the long-term goal is to provide residents with alternatives to car travel.

stories.headline: Red Flag Warning issued for Denver metro Monday as temperatures near record 80 degrees
stories.summary:
A Red Flag Warning for critical fire weather conditions has been issued for the Denver metro area and Front Range on Monday, as temperatures are expected to approach 80 degrees alongside afternoon wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph and very low humidity. Denver tied the 1879 record high of 79 degrees at DIA on Sunday; normal high temperatures for late March are around 60 degrees.

The combination of heat, wind, and dry conditions means any fires that ignite could spread rapidly, particularly along the foothills and nearby plains. Conditions are especially dangerous given recent dry weather and low snowpack across the region.

A cold front is forecast to move into the Denver area late Monday night into early Tuesday, bringing cooler temperatures and a chance of light precipitation on the plains. By Tuesday and Wednesday, increased moisture is expected, with snow likely in the mountains and a slight chance of a rain-snow mix on the plains overnight.

A brief warm-up is possible Thursday before another weather system could bring additional precipitation and cooler temperatures into next weekend.

stories.headline: Wildland fire contained at Buckley Space Force Base in Aurora Sunday morning
stories.summary:
A wildland fire burned Sunday morning on the southwest end of Buckley Space Force Base in Aurora, prompting a response from Aurora Fire Rescue and Aurora Police Department. The fire was contained and no longer posed a threat as of 10:53 a.m., according to Aurora Fire Rescue.

Smoke from the blaze was visible across much of Aurora, though no structures were threatened. The fire burned on base property east of Mississippi Avenue and Ceylon Street.

The incident comes amid an extended period of dry, warm weather across the Denver metro area and a Red Flag Warning for the region on Monday, heightening wildfire concerns throughout the Front Range.

weather.notable: About 26 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: My kids have two volumes: loud and louder. There is no mute button. I've checked.

topStory.headline: Trump Threatens to Seize or Destroy Iranian Oil Hub as U.S.-Iran Talks Stall
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump has raised the possibility of American forces seizing Iran's Kharg Island, the country's primary oil export terminal in the Persian Gulf, while simultaneously threatening to destroy the facility along with Iran's power plants, oil wells and desalination infrastructure if a negotiated settlement is not reached soon. Trump made the remarks in an interview with the Financial Times published Monday and in a separate social media post, saying the U.S. is engaged in talks with what he described as a 'new and more reasonable' Iranian regime. 'Maybe we take Kharg Island, maybe we don't. We have a lot of options,' Trump told the newspaper.

Iran's Foreign Ministry rejected Trump's characterization of the negotiations, with spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei stating that Tehran has had no direct talks with Washington and that U.S. demands conveyed through intermediaries have been 'excessive and unreasonable.' Baghaei said what has occurred so far amounts to messages passed through third parties, including Pakistan, and accused the U.S. of constantly shifting its position. Trump, however, said Iran had agreed to allow 20 ships carrying oil through the Strait of Hormuz starting Monday as 'a sign of respect,' a claim Tehran did not confirm.

The standoff over the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil shipping lane that Iran has blockaded during the conflict, remains the central pressure point in the dispute. Trump had previously set April 6 as a deadline for Iran to reopen the strait or face massive U.S. strikes on its energy infrastructure. Thousands of additional American troops have been deploying to the Middle East as the conflict expands.

The war has drawn in a widening circle of actors. Iran's Khatam al-Anbiya military command threatened to target the residences of American and Israeli military commanders and political officials in retaliation for what it called terrorist actions and strikes on Iranian residential areas. Iran's Revolutionary Guard separately warned that Israeli universities and U.S. university branches in the region could become 'legitimate targets' unless safety assurances for Iranian universities are provided. Spain also announced it has closed its airspace to U.S. military aircraft involved in the Iran conflict, with Defense Minister Margarita Robles saying the restriction had been communicated to American forces from the outset.

On a separate regional front, Iran's judiciary announced Monday it had begun revoking residency permits for approximately 1,200 UAE citizens living in Iran, a retaliatory move after the United Arab Emirates revoked residencies for Iranians and shuttered Iranian institutions in Dubai. The U.S.-Israel joint campaign has targeted several senior Iranian leadership figures, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and security chief Ali Larijani, drawing concern from the United Nations over the conflict's impact on civilians and civilian infrastructure. Whether direct negotiations will materialize before Trump's stated deadline remains unclear.
